Kamasutra

# Kamasutra

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Sep 06, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Kamasutra is generally believed to be written between 400 BCE and 200 CE, though some historians like John Keay state it to be around 2nd century CE. Now contrary to assumptions, Kama Sutra, is not really a sex manual, it is more a guide to living.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lVQW!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb382b5c4-62c0-48ad-a6d1-8ad243597d93_186x271.jpeg)

![The Complete Illustrated Kama Sutra ...](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lVQW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb382b5c4-62c0-48ad-a6d1-8ad243597d93_186x271.jpeg "The Complete Illustrated Kama Sutra ...")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Traditionally attributed to **Vatsyayana Mallanaga**, a philosopher believed to have lived around the **2nd century CE**, though estimates range from **400 BCE to 200 CE**, who himself clarifies that he is compiling earlier traditions, not inventing them—suggesting a long lineage of *Kama Shastra* thought.

The Kama Sutra is divided into **seven books** and **36 chapters**, with only **Book Two** focusing on sexual union. Kama is not lust—it is **refined desire**, the pursuit of beauty, emotional fulfillment, and relational harmony. Vatsyayana treats Kama as a **discipline**, akin to Dharma and Artha, requiring cultivation, ethics, and self-awareness. The text emphasizes **consent, mutual pleasure**, and emotional resonance—radically humane ideas for any era.

Victorian translators like **Sir Richard Burton** sensationalized the Kama Sutra, stripping it of its **philosophical and cultural depth**. Modern media often reduces it to **sexual positions**, ignoring its **aesthetic, emotional, and ethical sophistication**.

If we breakdown each and every chapter of Kama Sutra themewise

Part 1, has 5 chapters dealing with acquisition of knowledge, focusing on ethics, urbanity, goals of life, refined conduct.

Part 2, has 10 chapters, and this is one dealing with love making, that gets excessive attention.

Part 3, has 5 chapters, dealing with marriage, rituals and compatiblity.

Part 4, has 2 chapters dealing with domestic harmony, conduct of wife and husband.

Part 5, has 6 chapters dealing with social dynamics,courtship, psychology.

Part 6, has 6 chapters dealing with courtesans, their education, agency.

Part 7, has 2 chapters dealing with physical attraction, grooming.

***Now coming to the question, did it really reflect the society then? Does it represent India at it’s most affluent and openly hedonistic? I would say not exactly.***

Indian philosophy from the ages, revolved around the purusharthas or the 4 main goals of life- Dharma( Virtue), Artha( Wealth), Kama( Pleasure) and Moksha( Emancipation or Liberation). While Dharma, Artha and Kama are goals of normal life, Moksha represents a very high stage of release from the eternal cycle of rebirth and death. Sir Richard Burton’s translation of Kama Sutra sums this up rather well.

> *Dharma is better than Artha, and Artha is better than Kama. But Artha
> should always be first practised by the king for the livelihood of men
> is to be obtained from it only. Again, Kama being the occupation of
> public women, they should prefer it to the other two, and these are
> exceptions to the general rule.*

Typically Dharma is considered the highest aim in life, followed by Artha, as only wealth can give one a stable and secure life. Kama is generally the lowest of all aims. While pleasure is a part of life, and has to be enjoyed, hedonism is not something one should really aspire for. Even Vatsayayana himself states, that while youth is the time for pleasure, as the years pass by, a person should focus on how to make a living( Artha) following the path of virtue( Dharma).

Another quote from the Kamasutra states

> *Just as a horse in full gallop, blinded by the energy of his own
> speed, pays no attention to any post or hole or ditch on the path, so
> two lovers, blinded by passion, in the friction of sexual battle, are
> caught up in their fierce energy and pay no attention to danger.*

Kama Sutra is not a hymn to hedonism—it’s a meditation on balance. The Kama Sutra does not glorify unrestrained indulgence. It celebrates pleasure, yes—but only as one strand in the tapestry of life. Vatsyayana warns against lust that blinds, passion that disrupts Dharma, and desire that consumes Artha.

Also regarding whether it represented the Indian society of those times, it is again not a straightforward answer.

Just as **Tolstoy’s Anna Karenina** reflects the **Russian aristocracy**, not peasant life, and **Lawrence’s Lady Chatterley’s Lover** explores **English upper-class sensuality**, not working-class struggles, So too, **Vatsyayana’s Kama Sutra** reflects the **urban, Sanskrit-literate elite**, not the full spectrum of Indic society.

That is because literature per se is more about an author showcasing his or her thoughts. And that comes from the circle in which they move about.

Kama Sutra was written for the **well-bred townsman**, not the ascetic, villager, or tribal. Its concerns—**refined conduct, aesthetic pleasure, emotional strategy**—are elite preoccupations. It’s a **manual of cultivated living**, not a chronicle of mass behavior.

Erotica is inherently private. Love-making, desire, and emotional intimacy are **domestic acts**, not public rituals. The *Kama Sutra* explores **private aesthetics**, not **public customs**.Its vivid descriptions of postures and pleasure are **philosophical**, not **sociological**.

Public orgies or elaborate sensual rituals may have occurred in **elite circles**—wealthy nobles, merchants, courtesan salons. But these were **exceptions**, not **norms**. The *Kama Sutra* reflects **possibilities**, not **prevalence.**

The *Kama Sutra* does not represent a **hedonistic society**, nor does it preach **ascetic denial**. It offers a **middle ground**—a life where **pleasure is honored**, but **never worshipped**. Desire is treated as **a part of life**, not **its purpose**.

The *Kama Sutra* reflects the **urban elite**, not the **entire Indic society**.Social mores vary—**what is permissible in palaces** may be **taboo in villages and homes**. Literature, by nature, **amplifies the author’s world**, not the full spectrum of society.

Vatsyayana celebrates **refined pleasure**, but warns against **unrestrained lust**.He advocates **self-control**, **timing**, and **ethical boundaries**. The text is a **civilizational offering**, not a license for indulgence
